title: Aeneid, Book 8
source: Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th c.) via Vatican Library IIIF + Perseus Digital Library Latin (Greenough ed.) + Dryden English (1697, PG #228)
description: Book 8 of Virgil's Aeneid. Manuscript folio from Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225), the oldest surviving illustrated manuscript of Virgil (4th-5th century AD), held at the Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana. Latin text verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain). English translation verbatim from John Dryden's verse rendering (1697, Project Gutenberg #228, public domain).
introduction:
This entry presents Book 8 of Virgil's Aeneid with the strict three-criteria standard of the Theosis Library:

  1. Manuscript scan: A folio from the Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th century), one of the oldest and most important surviving Virgil manuscripts, with painted illustrations in the late antique Roman style. Via Vatican Library IIIF (digi.vatlib.it).
  2. Original text: The complete Latin text of Aeneid Book 8 (731 lines), verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain).
  3. English translation: John Dryden's celebrated verse translation (1697), verbatim from Project Gutenberg #228 (public domain).

translation.original_text: <sup>1</sup>Ut belli signum Laurenti Turnus ab arce <sup>2</sup>extulit et rauco strepuerunt cornua cantu, <sup>3</sup>utque acris concussit equos utque impulit arma, <sup>4</sup>extemplo turbati animi, simul omne tumultu <sup>5</sup>coniurat trepido Latium saevitque iuventus <sup>6</sup>effera. Ductores primi Messapus et Ufens <sup>7</sup>contemptorque deum Mezentius undique cogunt <sup>8</sup>auxilia et latos vastant cultoribus agros. <sup>9</sup>mittitur et magni Venulus Diomedis ad urbem, <sup>10</sup>qui petat auxilium et Latio consistere Teucros, <sup>11</sup>advectum Aenean classi victosque penatis <sup>12</sup>inferre et fatis regem se dicere posci <sup>13</sup>edoceat multasque viro se adiungere gentis <sup>14</sup>Dardanio et late Latio increbrescere nomen. <sup>15</sup>Quid struat his coeptis, quem, si Fortuna sequatur, <sup>16</sup>eventum pugnae cupiat, manifestius ipsi <sup>17</sup>quam Turno regi aut regi apparere Latino. <sup>18</sup>Talia per Latium. Quae Laomedontius heros <sup>19</sup>cuncta videns magno curarum fluctuat aestu, <sup>20</sup>atque animum nunc huc celerem, nunc dividit illuc. <sup>21</sup>In partisque rapit varias perque omnia versat: <sup>22</sup>sicut aquae tremulum labris ubi lumen aenis <sup>23</sup>sole repercussum aut radiantis imagine lunae <sup>24</sup>omnia pervolitat late loca iamque sub auras <sup>25</sup>erigitur summique ferit lacuaria tecti.

<sup>26</sup>Nox erat, et terras animalia fessa per omnis <sup>27</sup>alituum pecudumque genus sopor altus habebat: <sup>28</sup>cum pater in ripa gelidique sub aetheris axe <sup>29</sup>Aeneas, tristi turbatus pectora bello, <sup>30</sup>procubuit seramque dedit per membra quietem. <sup>31</sup>Huic deus ipse loci fluvio Tiberinus amoeno <sup>32</sup>populeas inter senior se attollere frondes <sup>33</sup>visus; eum tenuis glauco velabat amictu <sup>34</sup>carbasus, et crinis umbrosa tegebat harundo, <sup>35</sup>tum sic adfari et curas his demere dictis: <sup>36</sup>O sate gente deum, Troianam ex hostibus urbem <sup>37</sup>qui revehis nobis aeternaque Pergama servas, <sup>38</sup>exspectate solo Laurenti arvisque Latinis, <sup>39</sup>hic tibi certa domus, certi, ne absiste, penates; <sup>40</sup>neu belli terrere minis: tumor omnis et irae <sup>41</sup>concessere deum. <sup>42</sup>Iamque tibi, ne vana putes haec fingere somnum, <sup>43</sup>litoreis ingens inventa sub ilicibus sus, <sup>44</sup>triginta capitum fetus enixa, iacebit, <sup>45</sup>alba, solo recubans, albi circum ubera nati. <sup>46</sup>Hic locus urbis erit, requies ea certa laborum <sup>47</sup>ex quo ter denis urbem redeuntibus annis <sup>48</sup>Ascanius clari condet cognominis Albam. <sup>49</sup>Haud incerta cano. Nunc qua ratione quod instat <sup>50</sup>expedias victor, paucis (adverte) docebo.
translation.text: The war being now begun, both the generals make all possible preparations. Turnus sends to Diomedes. Aeneas goes in person to beg succours from Evander and the Tuscans. Evander receives him kindly, furnishes him with men, and sends his son Pallas with him. Vulcan, at the request of Venus, makes arms for her son Aeneas, and draws on his shield the most memorable actions of his posterity.

translation.original_text: <sup>51</sup>Arcades his oris, genus a Pallante profectum, <sup>52</sup>qui regem Euandrum comites, qui signa secuti, <sup>53</sup>delegere locum et posuere in montibus urbem <sup>54</sup>Pallantis proavi de nomine Pallanteum. <sup>55</sup>Hi bellum adsidue ducunt cum gente Latina; <sup>56</sup>hos castris adhibe socios et foedera iunge. <sup>57</sup>Ipse ego te ripis et recto flumine ducam, <sup>58</sup>adversum remis superes subvectus ut amnem. <sup>59</sup>Surge age, nate dea, primisque cadentibus astris <sup>60</sup>Iunoni fer rite preces iramque minasque <sup>61</sup>supplicibus supera votis. Mihi victor honorem <sup>62</sup>persolves. Ego sum pleno quem flumine cernis <sup>63</sup>stringentem ripas et pinguia culta secantem, <sup>64</sup>caeruleus Thybris, caelo gratissimus amnis. <sup>65</sup>Hic mihi magna domus, celsis caput urbibus, exit. <sup>66</sup>Dixit, deinde lacu fluvius se condidit alto, <sup>67</sup>ima petens; nox Aenean somnusque reliquit. <sup>68</sup>Surgit et aetherii spectans orientia solis <sup>69</sup>lumina rite cavis undam de flumine palmis <sup>70</sup>sustinet ac talis effundit ad aethera voces: <sup>71</sup>Nymphae, Laurentes nymphae, genus amnibus unde est, <sup>72</sup>tuque, o Thybri tuo genitor cum flumine sancto, <sup>73</sup>accipite Aenean et tandem arcete periclis. <sup>74</sup>Quo te cumque lacus miserantem incommoda nostra <sup>75</sup>fonte tenet, quocumque solo pulcherrimus exis,
translation.text: When Turnus had assembled all his pow’rs, His standard planted on Laurentum’s tow’rs; When now the sprightly trumpet, from afar, Had giv’n the signal of approaching war, Had rous’d the neighing steeds to scour the fields, While the fierce riders clatter’d on their shields; Trembling with rage, the Latian youth prepare To join th’ allies, and headlong rush to war. Fierce Ufens, and Messapus, led the crowd, With bold Mezentius, who blasphem’d aloud. These thro’ the country took their wasteful course, The fields to forage, and to gather force. Then Venulus to Diomede they send, To beg his aid Ausonia to defend, Declare the common danger, and inform The Grecian leader of the growing storm: “Aeneas, landed on the Latian coast, With banish’d gods, and with a baffled host, Yet now aspir’d to conquest of the state, And claim’d a title from the gods and fate; What num’rous nations in his quarrel came, And how they spread his formidable name. What he design’d, what mischief might arise, If fortune favour’d his first enterprise, Was left for him to weigh, whose equal fears, And common interest, was involv’d in theirs.”

translation.original_text: <sup>76</sup>semper honore meo, semper celebrabere donis <sup>77</sup>corniger hesperidum fluvius regnator aquarum. <sup>78</sup>Adsis o tantum et propius tua numina firmes. <sup>79</sup>sic memorat geminasque legit de classe biremis <sup>80</sup>remigioque aptat, socios simul instruit armis. <sup>81</sup>Ecce autem subitum atque oculis mirabile monstrum; <sup>82</sup>candida per silvam cum fetu concolor albo <sup>83</sup>procubuit viridique in litore conspicitur sus. <sup>84</sup>Quam pius Aeneas tibi enim, tibi, maxuma Iuno, <sup>85</sup>mactat sacra ferens et cum grege sistit ad aram. <sup>86</sup>Thybris ea fluvium, quam longa est, nocte tumentem <sup>87</sup>leniit, et tacita refluens ita substitit unda, <sup>88</sup>mitis ut in morem stagni placidaeque paludis <sup>89</sup>sterneret aequor aquis, remo ut luctamen abesset. <sup>90</sup>Ergo iter inceptum celerant rumore secundo; <sup>91</sup>labitur uncta vadis abies; mirantur et undae, <sup>92</sup>miratur nemus insuetum fulgentia longe <sup>93</sup>scuta virum fluvio pictasque innare carinas. <sup>94</sup>Olli remigio noctemque diemque fatigant <sup>95</sup>et longos superant flexus variisque teguntur <sup>96</sup>arboribus viridisque secant placido aequore silvas. <sup>97</sup>Sol medium caeli conscenderat igneus orbem, <sup>98</sup>cum muros arcemque procul ac rara domorum <sup>99</sup>tecta vident, quae nunc Romana potentia caelo <sup>100</sup>aequavit, tum res inopes Euandrus habebat:
translation.text: While Turnus and th’ allies thus urge the war, The Trojan, floating in a flood of care, Beholds the tempest which his foes prepare. This way and that he turns his anxious mind; Thinks, and rejects the counsels he design’d; Explores himself in vain, in ev’ry part, And gives no rest to his distracted heart. So, when the sun by day, or moon by night, Strike on the polish’d brass their trembling light, The glitt’ring species here and there divide, And cast their dubious beams from side to side; Now on the walls, now on the pavement play, And to the ceiling flash the glaring day.

translation.original_text: <sup>101</sup>ocius advertunt proras urbique propinquant. <sup>102</sup>Forte die sollemnem illo rex Arcas honorem <sup>103</sup>Amphitryoniadae magno divisque ferebat <sup>104</sup>ante urbem in luco. Pallas huic filius una, <sup>105</sup>una omnes iuvenum primi pauperque senatus <sup>106</sup>tura dabant, tepidusque cruor fumabat ad aras. <sup>107</sup>Ut celsas videre rates atque inter opacum <sup>108</sup>adlabi nemus et tacitis incumbere remis, <sup>109</sup>terrentur visu subito cunctique relictis <sup>110</sup>consurgunt mensis. Audax quos rumpere Pallas <sup>111</sup>sacra vetat raptoque volat telo obvius ipse <sup>112</sup>et procul e tumulo: Iuvenes, quae causa subegit <sup>113</sup>ignotas temptare vias, quo tenditis? inquit. <sup>114</sup>Qui genus? Unde domo? Pacemne huc fertis an arma? <sup>115</sup>Tum pater Aeneas puppi sic fatur ab alta <sup>116</sup>paciferaeque manu ramum praetendit olivae: <sup>117</sup>Troiugenas ac tela vides inimica Latinis, <sup>118</sup>quos illi bello profugos egere superbo. <sup>119</sup>Euandrum petimus. Ferte haec et dicite lectos <sup>120</sup>Dardaniae venisse duces socia arma rogantis. <sup>121</sup>Obstipuit tanto percussus nomine Pallas: <sup>122</sup>Egredere o quicumque es ait coramque parentem <sup>123</sup>adloquere ac nostris succede penatibus hospes. <sup>124</sup>excepitque manu dextramque amplexus inhaesit. <sup>125</sup>Progressi subeunt luco fluviumque relinquunt.
translation.text: ’Twas night; and weary nature lull’d asleep The birds of air, and fishes of the deep, And beasts, and mortal men. The Trojan chief Was laid on Tiber’s banks, oppress’d with grief, And found in silent slumber late relief. Then, thro’ the shadows of the poplar wood, Arose the father of the Roman flood; An azure robe was o’er his body spread, A wreath of shady reeds adorn’d his head: Thus, manifest to sight, the god appear’d, And with these pleasing words his sorrow cheer’d: “Undoubted offspring of ethereal race, O long expected in this promis’d place! Who thro’ the foes hast borne thy banish’d gods, Restor’d them to their hearths, and old abodes; This is thy happy home, the clime where fate Ordains thee to restore the Trojan state. Fear not! The war shall end in lasting peace, And all the rage of haughty Juno cease. And that this nightly vision may not seem Th’ effect of fancy, or an idle dream, A sow beneath an oak shall lie along, All white herself, and white her thirty young. When thirty rolling years have run their race, Thy son Ascanius, on this empty space, Shall build a royal town, of lasting fame, Which from this omen shall receive the name. Time shall approve the truth. For what remains, And how with sure success to crown thy pains, With patience next attend. A banish’d band, Driv’n with Evander from th’ Arcadian land, Have planted here, and plac’d on high their walls; Their town the founder Pallanteum calls, Deriv’d from Pallas, his great-grandsire’s name: But the fierce Latians old possession claim, With war infesting the new colony. These make thy friends, and on their aid rely. To thy free passage I submit my streams. Wake, son of Venus, from thy pleasing dreams; And, when the setting stars are lost in day, To Juno’s pow’r thy just devotion pay; With sacrifice the wrathful queen appease: Her pride at length shall fall, her fury cease. When thou return’st victorious from the war, Perform thy vows to me with grateful care. The god am I, whose yellow water flows Around these fields, and fattens as it goes: Tiber my name; among the rolling floods Renown’d on earth, esteem’d among the gods. This is my certain seat. In times to come, My waves shall wash the walls of mighty Rome.”

translation.original_text: <sup>126</sup>Tum regem Aeneas dictis adfatur amicis: <sup>127</sup>Optume Graiugenum, cui me Fortuna precari <sup>128</sup>et vitta comptos voluit praetendere ramos, <sup>129</sup>non equidem extimui, Danaum quod ductor et Arcas <sup>130</sup>quodque a stirpe fores geminis coniunctus Atridis; <sup>131</sup>sed mea me virtus et sancta oracula divom <sup>132</sup>cognatique patres, tua terris didita fama, <sup>133</sup>coniunxere tibi et fatis egere volentem. <sup>134</sup>Dardanus, Iliacae primus pater urbis et auctor, <sup>135</sup>Electra, ut Grai perhibent, Atlantide cretus, <sup>136</sup>advehitur Teucros; Electram maxumus Atlas <sup>137</sup>edidit, aetherios umero qui sustinet orbes <sup>138</sup>vobis Mercurius pater est, quem candida Maia <sup>139</sup>Cyllenae gelido conceptum vertice fudit; <sup>140</sup>at Maiam, auditis si quicquam credimus, Atlas, <sup>141</sup>idem Atlas generat, caeli qui sidera tollit. <sup>142</sup>Sic genus amborum scindit se sanguine ab uno. <sup>143</sup>His fretus non legatos neque prima per artem <sup>144</sup>temptamenta tui pepigi: me, me ipse meumque <sup>145</sup>obieci caput et supplex ad limina veni. <sup>146</sup>Gens eadem, quae te, crudeli Daunia bello <sup>147</sup>insequitur; nos si pellant, nihil afore credunt, <sup>148</sup>quin omnem Hesperiam penitus sua sub iuga mittant <sup>149</sup>et mare quod supra teneant quodque adluit infra. <sup>150</sup>Accipe daque fidem: sunt nobis fortia bello
translation.text: He said, and plung’d below. While yet he spoke, His dream Aeneas and his sleep forsook. He rose, and looking up, beheld the skies With purple blushing, and the day arise. Then water in his hollow palm he took From Tiber’s flood, and thus the pow’rs bespoke: “Laurentian nymphs, by whom the streams are fed, And Father Tiber, in thy sacred bed Receive Aeneas, and from danger keep. Whatever fount, whatever holy deep, Conceals thy wat’ry stores; where’er they rise, And, bubbling from below, salute the skies; Thou, king of horned floods, whose plenteous urn Suffices fatness to the fruitful corn, For this thy kind compassion of our woes, Shalt share my morning song and ev’ning vows. But, O be present to thy people’s aid, And firm the gracious promise thou hast made!” Thus having said, two galleys from his stores, With care he chooses, mans, and fits with oars. Now on the shore the fatal swine is found. Wond’rous to tell!—She lay along the ground: Her well-fed offspring at her udders hung; She white herself, and white her thirty young. Aeneas takes the mother and her brood, And all on Juno’s altar are bestow’d.

translation.original_text: <sup>151</sup>pectora, sunt animi et rebus spectata iuventus. <sup>152</sup>Dixerat Aeneas. Ille os oculosque loquentis <sup>153</sup>iamdudum et totum lustrabat lumine corpus. <sup>154</sup>Tum sic pauca refert: Ut te, fortissime Teucrum, <sup>155</sup>accipio agnoscoque libens! Ut verba parentis <sup>156</sup>et vocem Anchisae magni voltumque recordor! <sup>157</sup>Nam memini Hesionae visentem regna sororis <sup>158</sup>Laomedontiaden Priamum, Salamina petentem, <sup>159</sup>protinus Arcadiae gelidos invisere finis. <sup>160</sup>Tum mihi prima genas vestibat flore iuventas, <sup>161</sup>mirabarque duces Teucros, mirabar et ipsum <sup>162</sup>Laomedontiaden, sed cunctis altior ibat <sup>163</sup>Anchises: mihi mens iuvenali ardebat amore <sup>164</sup>compellare virum et dextrae coniungere dextram. <sup>165</sup>Accessi et cupidus Phenei sub moenia duxi. <sup>166</sup>Ille mihi insignem pharetram Lyciasque sagittas <sup>167</sup>discedens chlamydemque auro dedit intertextam <sup>168</sup>frenaque bina meus quae nunc habet aurea Pallas. <sup>169</sup>Ergo et quam petitis iuncta est mihi foedere dextra, <sup>170</sup>et lux cum primum terris se crastina reddet, <sup>171</sup>auxilio laetos dimittam opibusque iuvabo. <sup>172</sup>Interea sacra haec, quando huc venistis amici, <sup>173</sup>annua, quae differre nefas, celebrate faventes <sup>174</sup>nobiscum et iam nunc sociorum adsuescite mensis. <sup>175</sup>Haec ubi dicta, dapes iubet et sublata reponi
translation.text: The foll’wing night, and the succeeding day, Propitious Tiber smooth’d his wat’ry way: He roll’d his river back, and pois’d he stood, A gentle swelling, and a peaceful flood. The Trojans mount their ships; they put from shore, Borne on the waves, and scarcely dip an oar. Shouts from the land give omen to their course, And the pitch’d vessels glide with easy force. The woods and waters wonder at the gleam Of shields, and painted ships that stem the stream. One summer’s night and one whole day they pass Betwixt the greenwood shades, and cut the liquid glass. The fiery sun had finish’d half his race, Look’d back, and doubted in the middle space, When they from far beheld the rising tow’rs, The tops of sheds, and shepherds’ lowly bow’rs, Thin as they stood, which, then of homely clay, Now rise in marble, from the Roman sway. These cots (Evander’s kingdom, mean and poor) The Trojan saw, and turn’d his ships to shore. ’Twas on a solemn day: th’ Arcadian states, The king and prince, without the city gates, Then paid their off’rings in a sacred grove To Hercules, the warrior son of Jove. Thick clouds of rolling smoke involve the skies, And fat of entrails on his altar fries.

translation.original_text: <sup>176</sup>pocula gramineoque viros locat ipse sedili <sup>177</sup>praecipuumque toro et villosi pelle leonis <sup>178</sup>accipit Aenean solioque invitat acerno. <sup>179</sup>Tum lecti iuvenes certatim araeque sacerdos <sup>180</sup>viscera tosta ferunt taurorum onerantque canistris <sup>181</sup>dona laboratae Cereris Bacchumque ministrant. <sup>182</sup>Vescitur Aeneas simul et Troiana iuventus <sup>183</sup>perpetui tergo bovis et lustralibus extis. <sup>184</sup>Postquam exempta fames et amor compressus edendi, <sup>185</sup>rex Euandrus ait: Non haec sollemnia nobis, <sup>186</sup>has ex more dapes, hanc tanti numinis aram <sup>187</sup>vana superstitio veterumque ignara deorum <sup>188</sup>inposuit: saevis, hospes Troiane, periclis <sup>189</sup>servati facimus meritosque novamus honores. <sup>190</sup>Iam primum saxis suspensam hanc aspice rupem, <sup>191</sup>disiectae procul ut moles desertaque montis <sup>192</sup>stat domus et scopuli ingentem traxere ruinam. <sup>193</sup>Hic spelunca fuit, vasto summota recessu, <sup>194</sup>semihominis Caci facies quam dira tenebat <sup>195</sup>solis inaccessam radiis; semperque recenti <sup>196</sup>caede tepebat humus, foribusque adfixa superbis <sup>197</sup>ora virum tristi pendebant pallida tabo. <sup>198</sup>Huic monstro Volcanus erat pater: illius atros <sup>199</sup>ore vomens ignis magna se mole ferebat. <sup>200</sup>Attulit et nobis aliquando optantibus aetas
translation.text: But, when they saw the ships that stemm’d the flood, And glitter’d thro’ the covert of the wood, They rose with fear, and left th’ unfinish’d feast, Till dauntless Pallas reassur’d the rest To pay the rites. Himself without delay A jav’lin seiz’d, and singly took his way; Then gain’d a rising ground, and call’d from far: “Resolve me, strangers, whence, and what you are; Your bus’ness here; and bring you peace or war?” High on the stern Aeneas took his stand, And held a branch of olive in his hand, While thus he spoke: “The Phrygians’ arms you see, Expell’d from Troy, provok’d in Italy By Latian foes, with war unjustly made; At first affianc’d, and at last betray’d. This message bear: ‘The Trojans and their chief Bring holy peace, and beg the king’s relief.’ Struck with so great a name, and all on fire, The youth replies: “Whatever you require, Your fame exacts. Upon our shores descend. A welcome guest, and, what you wish, a friend.” He said, and, downward hasting to the strand, Embrac’d the stranger prince, and join’d his hand.

translation.original_text: <sup>201</sup>auxilium adventumque dei. Nam maximus ultor, <sup>202</sup>tergemini nece Geryonae spoliisque superbus <sup>203</sup>Alcides aderat taurosque hac victor agebat <sup>204</sup>ingentis, vallemque boves amnemque tenebant. <sup>205</sup>At furiis Caci mens effera, nequid inausum <sup>206</sup>aut intractatum scelerisve dolive fuisset, <sup>207</sup>quattuor a stabulis praestanti corpore tauros <sup>208</sup>avertit, totidem forma superante iuvencas; <sup>209</sup>atque hos, nequa forent pedibus vestigia rectis, <sup>210</sup>cauda in speluncam tractos versisque viarum <sup>211</sup>indiciis raptos saxo occultabat opaco: <sup>212</sup>quaerenti nulla ad speluncam signa ferebant. <sup>213</sup>Interea, cum iam stabulis saturata moveret <sup>214</sup>Amphytrioniades armenta abitumque pararet, <sup>215</sup>discessu mugire boves atque omne querelis <sup>216</sup>impleri nemus et colles clamore relinqui. <sup>217</sup>reddidit una boum vocem vastoque sub antro <sup>218</sup>mugiit et Caci spem custodita fefellit. <sup>219</sup>Hic vero Alcidae furiis exarserat atro <sup>220</sup>felle dolor: rapit arma manu nodisque gravatum <sup>221</sup>robur et aerii cursu petit ardua montis. <sup>222</sup>Tum primum nostri Cacum videre timentem <sup>223</sup>turbatumque oculis: fugit ilicet ocior Euro <sup>224</sup>speluncamque petit, pedibus timor addidit alas. <sup>225</sup>Ut sese inclusit ruptisque immane catenis
translation.text: Conducted to the grove, Aeneas broke The silence first, and thus the king bespoke: “Best of the Greeks, to whom, by fate’s command, I bear these peaceful branches in my hand, Undaunted I approach you, tho’ I know Your birth is Grecian, and your land my foe; From Atreus tho’ your ancient lineage came, And both the brother kings your kindred claim; Yet, my self-conscious worth, your high renown, Your virtue, thro’ the neighb’ring nations blown, Our fathers’ mingled blood, Apollo’s voice, Have led me hither, less by need than choice. Our founder Dardanus, as fame has sung, And Greeks acknowledge, from Electra sprung: Electra from the loins of Atlas came; Atlas, whose head sustains the starry frame. Your sire is Mercury, whom long before On cold Cyllene’s top fair Maia bore. Maia the fair, on fame if we rely, Was Atlas’ daughter, who sustains the sky. Thus from one common source our streams divide; Ours is the Trojan, yours th’ Arcadian side. Rais’d by these hopes, I sent no news before, Nor ask’d your leave, nor did your faith implore; But come, without a pledge, my own ambassador. The same Rutulians, who with arms pursue The Trojan race, are equal foes to you. Our host expell’d, what farther force can stay The victor troops from universal sway? Then will they stretch their pow’r athwart the land, And either sea from side to side command. Receive our offer’d faith, and give us thine; Ours is a gen’rous and experienc’d line: We want not hearts nor bodies for the war; In council cautious, and in fields we dare.”

translation.original_text: <sup>226</sup>deiecit saxum, ferro quod et arte paterna <sup>227</sup>pendebat, fultosque emuniit obice postis, <sup>228</sup>ecce furens animis aderat Tirynthius omnemque <sup>229</sup>accessum lustrans huc ora ferebat et illuc, <sup>230</sup>dentibus infrendens. Ter totum fervidus ira <sup>231</sup>lustrat Aventini montem, ter saxea temptat <sup>232</sup>limina nequiquam, ter fessus valle resedit. <sup>233</sup>Stabat acuta silex, praecisis undique saxis <sup>234</sup>speluncae dorso insurgens, altissima visu, <sup>235</sup>dirarum nidis domus opportuna volucrum. <sup>236</sup>Hanc, ut prona iugo laevum incumbebat in amnem, <sup>237</sup>dexter in adversum nitens concussit et imis <sup>238</sup>avolsam solvit radicibus; inde repente <sup>239</sup>inpulit, inpulsu quo maximus intonat aether <sup>240</sup>dissultant ripae refluitque exterritus amnis. <sup>241</sup>At specus et Caci detecta apparuit ingens <sup>242</sup>regia, et umbrosae penitus patuere cavernae: <sup>243</sup>non secus ac siqua penitus vi terra dehiscens <sup>244</sup>infernas reseret sedes et regna recludat <sup>245</sup>pallida, dis invisa, superque immane barathrum <sup>246</sup>cernatur, trepident inmisso lumine manes. <sup>247</sup>Ergo insperata deprensum luce repente <sup>248</sup>inclusumque cavo saxo atque insueta rudentem <sup>249</sup>desuper Alcides telis premit omniaque arma <sup>250</sup>advocat et ramis vastisque molaribus instat.

<sup>251</sup>Ille autem, neque enim fuga iam super ulla pericli, <sup>252</sup>faucibus ingentem fumum (mirabile dictu) <sup>253</sup>evomit involvitque domum caligine caeca, <sup>254</sup>prospectum eripiens oculis, glomeratque sub antro <sup>255</sup>fumiferam noctem commixtis igne tenebris. <sup>256</sup>Non tulit Alcides animis seque ipse per ignem <sup>257</sup>praecipiti iecit saltu, qua plurimus undam <sup>258</sup>fumus agit nebulaque ingens specus aestuat atra. <sup>259</sup>Hic Cacum in tenebris incendia vana vomentem <sup>260</sup>corripit in nodum complexus et angit inhaerens <sup>261</sup>elisos oculos et siccum sanguine guttur. <sup>262</sup>Panditur extemplo foribus domus atra revolsis, <sup>263</sup>abstractaeque boves abiurataeque rapinae <sup>264</sup>caelo ostenduntur, pedibusque informe cadaver <sup>265</sup>protrahitur. Nequeunt expleri corda tuendo <sup>266</sup>terribilis oculos, voltum villosaque saetis <sup>267</sup>pectora semiferi atque extinctos faucibus ignis. <sup>268</sup>Ex illo celebratus honos, laetique minores <sup>269</sup>servavere diem, primusque Potitius auctor <sup>270</sup>et domus Herculei custos Pinaria sacri. <sup>271</sup>Hanc aram luco statuit, quae maxuma semper <sup>272</sup>dicetur nobis et erit quae maxuma semper. <sup>273</sup>Quare agite, o iuvenes, tantarum in munere laudum <sup>274</sup>cingite fronde comas et pocula porgite dextris <sup>275</sup>communemque vocate deum et date vina volentes.
translation.text: He said; and while spoke, with piercing eyes Evander view’d the man with vast surprise, Pleas’d with his action, ravish’d with his face: Then answer’d briefly, with a royal grace: “O valiant leader of the Trojan line, In whom the features of thy father shine, How I recall Anchises! how I see His motions, mien, and all my friend, in thee! Long tho’ it be, ’tis fresh within my mind, When Priam to his sister’s court design’d A welcome visit, with a friendly stay, And thro’ th’ Arcadian kingdom took his way. Then, past a boy, the callow down began To shade my chin, and call me first a man. I saw the shining train with vast delight, And Priam’s goodly person pleas’d my sight: But great Anchises, far above the rest, With awful wonder fir’d my youthful breast. I long’d to join in friendship’s holy bands Our mutual hearts, and plight our mutual hands. I first accosted him: I sued, I sought, And, with a loving force, to Pheneus brought. He gave me, when at length constrain’d to go, A Lycian quiver and a Gnossian bow, A vest embroider’d, glorious to behold, And two rich bridles, with their bits of gold, Which my son’s coursers in obedience hold. The league you ask, I offer, as your right; And, when tomorrow’s sun reveals the light, With swift supplies you shall be sent away. Now celebrate with us this solemn day, Whose holy rites admit no long delay. Honour our annual feast; and take your seat, With friendly welcome, at a homely treat.” Thus having said, the bowls remov’d (for fear) The youths replac’d, and soon restor’d the cheer. On sods of turf he set the soldiers round: A maple throne, rais’d higher from the ground, Receiv’d the Trojan chief; and, o’er the bed, A lion’s shaggy hide for ornament they spread. The loaves were serv’d in canisters; the wine In bowls; the priest renew’d the rites divine: Broil’d entrails are their food, and beef’s continued chine.

translation.original_text: <sup>276</sup>Dixerat, Herculea bicolor cum populus umbra <sup>277</sup>velavitque comas foliisque innexa pependit <sup>278</sup>et sacer implevit dextram scyphus. Ocius omnes <sup>279</sup>in mensam laeti libant divosque precantur. <sup>280</sup>Devexo interea propior fit Vesper Olympo, <sup>281</sup>iamque sacerdotes primusque Potitius ibant, <sup>282</sup>pellibus in morem cincti, flammasque ferebant. <sup>283</sup>Instaurant epulas et mensae grata secundae <sup>284</sup>dona ferunt cumulantque oneratis lancibus aras. <sup>285</sup>tum Salii ad cantus incensa altaria circum <sup>286</sup>populeis adsunt evincti tempora ramis, <sup>287</sup>hic iuvenum chorus, ille senum; qui carmine laudes <sup>288</sup>Herculeas et facta ferunt: ut prima novercae <sup>289</sup>monstra manu geminosque premens eliserit angues, <sup>290</sup>ut bello egregias idem disiecerit urbes, <sup>291</sup>Troiamque Oechaliamque, ut duros mille labores <sup>292</sup>rege sub Eurystheo fatis Iunonis iniquae <sup>293</sup>pertulerit. Tu nubigenas, invicte, bimembris <sup>294</sup>Hylaeeumque Pholumque, manu, tu Cresia mactas <sup>295</sup>prodigia et vastum Nemeae sub rupe leonem. <sup>296</sup>Te Stygii tremuere lacus, te ianitor Orci <sup>297</sup>ossa super recubans antro semesa cruento; <sup>298</sup>nec te ullae facies, non terruit ipse Typhoeus, <sup>299</sup>arduus arma tenens; non te rationis egentem <sup>300</sup>Lernaeus turba capitum circumstetit anguis.
translation.text: But when the rage of hunger was repress’d, Thus spoke Evander to his royal guest: “These rites, these altars, and this feast, O king, From no vain fears or superstition spring, Or blind devotion, or from blinder chance, Or heady zeal, or brutal ignorance; But, sav’d from danger, with a grateful sense, The labours of a god we recompense. See, from afar, yon rock that mates the sky, About whose feet such heaps of rubbish lie; Such indigested ruin; bleak and bare, How desert now it stands, expos’d in air! ’Twas once a robber’s den, inclos’d around With living stone, and deep beneath the ground. The monster Cacus, more than half a beast, This hold, impervious to the sun, possess’d. The pavement ever foul with human gore; Heads, and their mangled members, hung the door. Vulcan this plague begot; and, like his sire, Black clouds he belch’d, and flakes of livid fire. Time, long expected, eas’d us of our load, And brought the needful presence of a god. Th’ avenging force of Hercules, from Spain, Arriv’d in triumph, from Geryon slain: Thrice liv’d the giant, and thrice liv’d in vain. His prize, the lowing herds, Alcides drove Near Tiber’s bank, to graze the shady grove. Allur’d with hope of plunder, and intent By force to rob, by fraud to circumvent, The brutal Cacus, as by chance they stray’d, Four oxen thence, and four fair kine convey’d; And, lest the printed footsteps might be seen, He dragg’d ’em backwards to his rocky den. The tracks averse a lying notice gave, And led the searcher backward from the cave.

translation.original_text: <sup>301</sup>Salve, vera Iovis proles, decus addite divis, <sup>302</sup>et nos et tua dexter adi pede sacra secundo. <sup>303</sup>Talia carminibus celebrant; super omnia Caci <sup>304</sup>speluncam adiciunt spirantemque ignibus ipsum. <sup>305</sup>Consonat omne nemus strepitu collesque resultant. <sup>306</sup>Exim se cuncti divinis rebus ad urbem <sup>307</sup>perfectis referunt. Ibat rex obsitus aevo <sup>308</sup>et comitem Aenean iuxta natumque tenebat <sup>309</sup>ingrediens varioque viam sermone levabat. <sup>310</sup>miratur facilisque oculos fert omnia circum <sup>311</sup>Aeneas capiturque locis et singula laetus <sup>312</sup>exquiritque auditque virum monimenta priorum. <sup>313</sup>Tum rex Euandrus, Romanae conditor arcis: <sup>314</sup>Haec nemora indigenae fauni nymphaeque tenebant <sup>315</sup>gensque virum truncis et duro robore nata, <sup>316</sup>quis neque mos neque cultus erat, nec iungere tauros <sup>317</sup>aut componere opes norant aut parcere parto, <sup>318</sup>sed rami atque asper victu venatus alebat. <sup>319</sup>Primus ab aetherio venit Saturnus Olympo, <sup>320</sup>arma Iovis fugiens et regnis exsul ademptis. <sup>321</sup>Is genus indocile ac dispersum montibus altis <sup>322</sup>composuit legesque dedit Latiumque vocari <sup>323</sup>maluit, his quoniam latuisset tutis in oris. <sup>324</sup>Aurea quae perhibent illo sub rege fuere <sup>325</sup>saecula. Sic placida populos in pace regebat,
translation.text: “Meantime the herdsman hero shifts his place, To find fresh pasture and untrodden grass. The beasts, who miss’d their mates, fill’d all around With bellowings, and the rocks restor’d the sound. One heifer, who had heard her love complain, Roar’d from the cave, and made the project vain. Alcides found the fraud; with rage he shook, And toss’d about his head his knotted oak. Swift as the winds, or Scythian arrows’ flight, He clomb, with eager haste, th’ aerial height. Then first we saw the monster mend his pace; Fear in his eyes, and paleness in his face, Confess’d the god’s approach. Trembling he springs, As terror had increas’d his feet with wings; Nor stay’d for stairs; but down the depth he threw His body, on his back the door he drew (The door, a rib of living rock; with pains His father hew’d it out, and bound with iron chains): He broke the heavy links, the mountain clos’d, And bars and levers to his foe oppos’d. The wretch had hardly made his dungeon fast; The fierce avenger came with bounding haste; Survey’d the mouth of the forbidden hold, And here and there his raging eyes he roll’d. He gnash’d his teeth; and thrice he compass’d round With winged speed the circuit of the ground. Thrice at the cavern’s mouth he pull’d in vain, And, panting, thrice desisted from his pain. A pointed flinty rock, all bare and black, Grew gibbous from behind the mountain’s back; Owls, ravens, all ill omens of the night, Here built their nests, and hither wing’d their flight. The leaning head hung threat’ning o’er the flood, And nodded to the left. The hero stood Adverse, with planted feet, and, from the right, Tugg’d at the solid stone with all his might. Thus heav’d, the fix’d foundations of the rock Gave way; heav’n echo’d at the rattling shock. Tumbling, it chok’d the flood: on either side The banks leap backward, and the streams divide; The sky shrunk upward with unusual dread, And trembling Tiber div’d beneath his bed. The court of Cacus stands reveal’d to sight; The cavern glares with new-admitted light. So the pent vapours, with a rumbling sound, Heave from below, and rend the hollow ground; A sounding flaw succeeds; and, from on high, The gods with hate beheld the nether sky: The ghosts repine at violated night, And curse th’ invading sun, and sicken at the sight. The graceless monster, caught in open day, Inclos’d, and in despair to fly away, Howls horrible from underneath, and fills His hollow palace with unmanly yells. The hero stands above, and from afar Plies him with darts, and stones, and distant war. He, from his nostrils huge mouth, expires Black clouds of smoke, amidst his father’s fires, Gath’ring, with each repeated blast, the night, To make uncertain aim, and erring sight. The wrathful god then plunges from above, And, where in thickest waves the sparkles drove, There lights; and wades thro’ fumes, and gropes his way, Half sing’d, half stifled, till he grasps his prey. The monster, spewing fruitless flames, he found; He squeez’d his throat; he writh’d his neck around, And in a knot his crippled members bound; Then from their sockets tore his burning eyes: Roll’d on a heap, the breathless robber lies. The doors, unbarr’d, receive the rushing day, And thoro’ lights disclose the ravish’d prey. The bulls, redeem’d, breathe open air again. Next, by the feet, they drag him from his den. The wond’ring neighbourhood, with glad surprise, Behold his shagged breast, his giant size, His mouth that flames no more, and his extinguish’d eyes. From that auspicious day, with rites divine, We worship at the hero’s holy shrine. Potitius first ordain’d these annual vows: As priests, were added the Pinarian house, Who rais’d this altar in the sacred shade, Where honours, ever due, for ever shall be paid. For these deserts, and this high virtue shown, Ye warlike youths, your heads with garlands crown: Fill high the goblets with a sparkling flood, And with deep draughts invoke our common god.”

translation.original_text: <sup>326</sup>deterior donec paulatim ac decolor aetas <sup>327</sup>et belli rabies et amor successit habendi. <sup>328</sup>Tum manus Ausonia et gentes venere Sicanae, <sup>329</sup>saepius et nomen posuit Saturnia tellus; <sup>330</sup>tum reges asperque immani corpore Thybris, <sup>331</sup>a quo post Itali fluvium cognomine Thybrim <sup>332</sup>diximus, amisit verum vetus Albula nomen; <sup>333</sup>me pulsum patria pelagique extrema sequentem <sup>334</sup>Fortuna omnipotens et ineluctabile fatum <sup>335</sup>his posuere locis matrisque egere tremenda <sup>336</sup>Carmentis nymphae monita et deus auctor Apollo. <sup>337</sup>Vix ea dicta: dehinc progressus monstrat et aram <sup>338</sup>et Carmentalem Romani nomine portam <sup>339</sup>quam memorant, nymphae priscum Carmentis honorem, <sup>340</sup>vatis fatidicae, cecinit quae prima futuros <sup>341</sup>Aeneadas magnos et nobile Pallanteum. <sup>342</sup>Hinc lucum ingentem quem Romulus acer Asylum <sup>343</sup>rettulit et gelida monstrat sub rupe Lupercal, <sup>344</sup>Parrhasio dictum Panos de more Lycaei. <sup>345</sup>Nec non et sacri monstrat nemus Argileti <sup>346</sup>testaturque locum et letum docet hospitis Argi. <sup>347</sup>Hinc ad Tarpeiam sedem et Capitolia ducit, <sup>348</sup>aurea nunc, olim silvestribus horrida dumis. <sup>349</sup>Iam tum religio pavidos terrebat agrestis <sup>350</sup>dira loci, iam tum silvam saxumque tremebant.
translation.text: This said, a double wreath Evander twin’d, And poplars black and white his temples bind. Then brims his ample bowl. With like design The rest invoke the gods, with sprinkled wine. Meantime the sun descended from the skies, And the bright evening star began to rise. And now the priests, Potitius at their head, In skins of beasts involv’d, the long procession led; Held high the flaming tapers in their hands, As custom had prescrib’d their holy bands; Then with a second course the tables load, And with full chargers offer to the god. The Salii sing, and cense his altars round With Saban smoke, their heads with poplar bound One choir of old, another of the young, To dance, and bear the burthen of the song. The lay records the labours, and the praise, And all th’ immortal acts of Hercules: First, how the mighty babe, when swath’d in bands, The serpents strangled with his infant hands; Then, as in years and matchless force he grew, Th’ Oechalian walls, and Trojan, overthrew. Besides, a thousand hazards they relate, Procur’d by Juno’s and Eurystheus’ hate: “Thy hands, unconquer’d hero, could subdue The cloud-born Centaurs, and the monster crew: Nor thy resistless arm the bull withstood, Nor he, the roaring terror of the wood. The triple porter of the Stygian seat, With lolling tongue, lay fawning at thy feet, And, seiz’d with fear, forgot his mangled meat. Th’ infernal waters trembled at thy sight; Thee, god, no face of danger could affright; Not huge Typhoeus, nor th’ unnumber’d snake, Increas’d with hissing heads, in Lerna’s lake. Hail, Jove’s undoubted son! an added grace To heav’n and the great author of thy race! Receive the grateful off’rings which we pay, And smile propitious on thy solemn day!” In numbers thus they sung; above the rest, The den and death of Cacus crown the feast. The woods to hollow vales convey the sound, The vales to hills, and hills the notes rebound. The rites perform’d, the cheerful train retire.

translation.original_text: <sup>351</sup>Hoc nemus, hunc, inquit, frondoso vertice collem <sup>352</sup>(quis deus incertum est) habitat deus: Arcades ipsum <sup>353</sup>credunt se vidisse Iovem, cum saepe nigrantem <sup>354</sup>aegida concuteret dextra nimbosque cieret. <sup>355</sup>Haec duo praeterea disiectis oppida muris, <sup>356</sup>reliquias veterumque vides monimenta virorum. <sup>357</sup>Hanc Ianus pater, hanc Saturnus condidit arcem: <sup>358</sup>Ianiculum huic, illi fuerat Saturnia nomen. <sup>359</sup>Talibus inter se dictis ad tecta subibant <sup>360</sup>pauperis Euandri passimque armenta videbant <sup>361</sup>Romanoque foro et lautis mugire Carinis. <sup>362</sup>Ut ventum ad sedes: Haec, inquit, limina victor <sup>363</sup>Alcides subiit, haec illum regia cepit. <sup>364</sup>Aude, hospes, contemnere opes et te quoque dignum <sup>365</sup>finge deo rebusque veni non asper egenis. <sup>366</sup>dixit et angusti subter fastigia tecti <sup>367</sup>ingentem Aenean duxit stratisque locavit <sup>368</sup>effultum foliis et pelle Libystidis ursae. <sup>369</sup>Nox ruit et fuscis tellurem amplectitur alis. <sup>370</sup>At Venus haud animo nequiquam exterrita mater <sup>371</sup>Laurentumque minis et duro mota tumultu <sup>372</sup>Volcanum adloquitur thalamoque haec coniugis aureo <sup>373</sup>incipit et dictis divinum adspirat amorem: <sup>374</sup>Dum bello Argolici vastabant Pergama reges <sup>375</sup>debita casurasque inimicis ignibus arces,
translation.text: Betwixt young Pallas and his aged sire, The Trojan pass’d, the city to survey, And pleasing talk beguil’d the tedious way. The stranger cast around his curious eyes, New objects viewing still, with new surprise; With greedy joy enquires of various things, And acts and monuments of ancient kings. Then thus the founder of the Roman tow’rs: “These woods were first the seat of sylvan pow’rs, Of Nymphs and Fauns, and salvage men, who took Their birth from trunks of trees and stubborn oak. Nor laws they knew, nor manners, nor the care Of lab’ring oxen, or the shining share, Nor arts of gain, nor what they gain’d to spare. Their exercise the chase; the running flood Supplied their thirst, the trees supplied their food. Then Saturn came, who fled the pow’r of Jove, Robb’d of his realms, and banish’d from above. The men, dispers’d on hills, to towns he brought, And laws ordain’d, and civil customs taught, And Latium call’d the land where safe he lay From his unduteous son, and his usurping sway. With his mild empire, peace and plenty came; And hence the golden times deriv’d their name. A more degenerate and discolour’d age Succeeded this, with avarice and rage. Th’ Ausonians then, and bold Sicanians came; And Saturn’s empire often chang’d the name. Then kings, gigantic Tybris, and the rest, With arbitrary sway the land oppress’d: For Tiber’s flood was Albula before, Till, from the tyrant’s fate, his name it bore. I last arriv’d, driv’n from my native home By fortune’s pow’r, and fate’s resistless doom. Long toss’d on seas, I sought this happy land, Warn’d by my mother nymph, and call’d by Heav’n’s command.”

translation.original_text: <sup>376</sup>non ullum auxilium miseris, non arma rogavi <sup>377</sup>artis opisque tuae nec te, carissime coniunx, <sup>378</sup>incassumve tuos volui exercere labores, <sup>379</sup>quamvis et Priami deberem plurima natis <sup>380</sup>et durum Aeneae flevissem saepe laborem. <sup>381</sup>Nunc Iovis imperiis Rutulorum constitit oris: <sup>382</sup>ergo eadem supplex venio et sanctum mihi numen <sup>383</sup>arma rogo genetrix nato. Te filia Nerei, <sup>384</sup>te potuit lacrimis Tithonia flectere coniunx. <sup>385</sup>Aspice qui coeant populi, quae moenia clausis <sup>386</sup>ferrum acuant portis in me exscidiumque meorum. <sup>387</sup>Dixerat et niveis hinc atque hinc diva lacertis <sup>388</sup>cunctantem amplexu molli fovet. Ille repente <sup>389</sup>accepit solitam flammam, notusque medullas <sup>390</sup>intravit calor et labefacta per ossa cucurrit: <sup>391</sup>non secus atque olim tonitru cum rupta corusco <sup>392</sup>ignea rima micans percurrit lumine nimbos. <sup>393</sup>Sensit laeta dolis et formae conscia coniunx. <sup>394</sup>Tum pater aeterno fatur devinctus amore: <sup>395</sup>Quid causas petis ex alto? Fiducia cessit <sup>396</sup>quo tibi, diva, mei? Similis si cura fuisset, <sup>397</sup>tum quoque fas nobis Teucros armare fuisset: <sup>398</sup>nec pater omnipotens Troiam nec fata vetabant <sup>399</sup>stare decemque alios Priamum superesse per annos. <sup>400</sup>Et nunc, si bellare paras atque haec tibi mens est,
translation.text: Thus, walking on, he spoke, and shew’d the gate, Since call’d Carmental by the Roman state; Where stood an altar, sacred to the name Of old Carmenta, the prophetic dame, Who to her son foretold th’ Aenean race, Sublime in fame, and Rome’s imperial place: Then shews the forest, which, in after times, Fierce Romulus for perpetrated crimes A sacred refuge made; with this, the shrine Where Pan below the rock had rites divine: Then tells of Argus’ death, his murder’d guest, Whose grave and tomb his innocence attest. Thence, to the steep Tarpeian rock he leads; Now roof’d with gold, then thatch’d with homely reeds. A reverent fear (such superstition reigns Among the rude) ev’n then possess’d the swains. Some god, they knew—what god, they could not tell— Did there amidst the sacred horror dwell. Th’ Arcadians thought him Jove; and said they saw The mighty Thund’rer with majestic awe, Who took his shield, and dealt his bolts around, And scatter’d tempests on the teeming ground. Then saw two heaps of ruins, (once they stood Two stately towns, on either side the flood,) Saturnia’s and Janiculum’s remains; And either place the founder’s name retains. Discoursing thus together, they resort Where poor Evander kept his country court. They view’d the ground of Rome’s litigious hall; (Once oxen low’d, where now the lawyers bawl;) Then, stooping, thro’ the narrow gate they press’d, When thus the king bespoke his Trojan guest: “Mean as it is, this palace, and this door, Receiv’d Alcides, then a conqueror. Dare to be poor; accept our homely food, Which feasted him, and emulate a god.” Then underneath a lowly roof he led The weary prince, and laid him on a bed; The stuffing leaves, with hides of bears o’erspread. Now night had shed her silver dews around, And with her sable wings embrac’d the ground, When love’s fair goddess, anxious for her son, (New tumults rising, and new wars begun,) Couch’d with her husband in his golden bed, With these alluring words invokes his aid; And, that her pleasing speech his mind may move, Inspires each accent with the charms of love: “While cruel fate conspir’d with Grecian pow’rs, To level with the ground the Trojan tow’rs, I ask’d not aid th’ unhappy to restore, Nor did the succour of thy skill implore; Nor urg’d the labours of my lord in vain, A sinking empire longer to sustain, Tho’ much I ow’d to Priam’s house, and more The dangers of Aeneas did deplore. But now, by Jove’s command, and fate’s decree, His race is doom’d to reign in Italy: With humble suit I beg thy needful art, O still propitious pow’r, that rules my heart! A mother kneels a suppliant for her son. By Thetis and Aurora thou wert won To forge impenetrable shields, and grace With fated arms a less illustrious race. Behold, what haughty nations are combin’d Against the relics of the Phrygian kind, With fire and sword my people to destroy, And conquer Venus twice, in conqu’ring Troy.” She said; and straight her arms, of snowy hue, About her unresolving husband threw. Her soft embraces soon infuse desire; His bones and marrow sudden warmth inspire; And all the godhead feels the wonted fire. Not half so swift the rattling thunder flies, Or forky lightnings flash along the skies. The goddess, proud of her successful wiles, And conscious of her form, in secret smiles.

translation.original_text: <sup>401</sup>quidquid in arte mea possum promittere curae, <sup>402</sup>quod fieri ferro liquidove potest electro, <sup>403</sup>quantum ignes animaeque valent, absiste precando <sup>404</sup>viribus indubitare tuis. Ea verba locutus <sup>405</sup>optatos dedit amplexus placidumque petivit <sup>406</sup>coniugis infusus gremio per membra soporem. <sup>407</sup>Inde ubi prima quies medio iam noctis abactae <sup>408</sup>curriculo expulerat somnum, cum femina primum, <sup>409</sup>cui tolerare colo vitam tenuique Minerva <sup>410</sup>impositum, cinerem et sopitos suscitat ignes, <sup>411</sup>noctem addens operi, famulasque ad lumina longo <sup>412</sup>exercet penso, castum ut servare cubile <sup>413</sup>coniugis et possit parvos educere natos: <sup>414</sup>haud secus Ignipotens nec tempore segnior illo <sup>415</sup>mollibus e stratis opera ad fabrilia surgit. <sup>416</sup>Insula Sicanium iuxta latus Aeoliamque <sup>417</sup>erigitur Liparen, fumantibus ardua saxis, <sup>418</sup>quam subter specus et Cyclopum exesa caminis <sup>419</sup>antra Aetnaea tonant validique incudibus ictus <sup>420</sup>auditi referunt gemitus striduntque cavernis <sup>421</sup>stricturae Chalybum et fornacibus ignis anhelat, <sup>422</sup>Volcani domus et Volcania nomine tellus. <sup>423</sup>Hoc tunc ignipotens caelo descendit ab alto. <sup>424</sup>Ferrum exercebant vasto Cyclopes in antro, <sup>425</sup>Brontesque Steropesque et nudus membra Pyragmon.
translation.text: Then thus the pow’r, obnoxious to her charms, Panting, and half dissolving in her arms: “Why seek you reasons for a cause so just, Or your own beauties or my love distrust? Long since, had you requir’d my helpful hand, Th’ artificer and art you might command, To labour arms for Troy: nor Jove, nor fate, Confin’d their empire to so short a date. And, if you now desire new wars to wage, My skill I promise, and my pains engage. Whatever melting metals can conspire, Or breathing bellows, or the forming fire, Is freely yours: your anxious fears remove, And think no task is difficult to love.” Trembling he spoke; and, eager of her charms, He snatch’d the willing goddess to his arms; Till in her lap infus’d, he lay possess’d Of full desire, and sunk to pleasing rest. Now when the night her middle race had rode, And his first slumber had refresh’d the god— The time when early housewives leave the bed; When living embers on the hearth they spread, Supply the lamp, and call the maids to rise;— With yawning mouths, and with half-open’d eyes, They ply the distaff by the winking light, And to their daily labour add the night: Thus frugally they earn their children’s bread, And uncorrupted keep the nuptial bed— Not less concern’d, nor at a later hour, Rose from his downy couch the forging pow’r.

translation.original_text: <sup>426</sup>His informatum manibus iam parte polita <sup>427</sup>fulmen erat, toto genitor quae plurima caelo <sup>428</sup>deicit in terras, pars inperfecta manebat. <sup>429</sup>Tris imbris torti radios, tris nubis aquosae <sup>430</sup>addiderant, rutili tris ignis et alitis austri: <sup>431</sup>fulgores nunc horrificos sonitumque metumque <sup>432</sup>miscebant operi flammisque sequacibus iras. <sup>433</sup>Parte alia Marti currumque rotasque volucris <sup>434</sup>instabant, quibus ille viros, quibus excitat urbes; <sup>435</sup>aegidaque horriferam, turbatae Palladis arma, <sup>436</sup>certatim squamis serpentum auroque polibant <sup>437</sup>conexosque anguis ipsamque in pectore divae <sup>438</sup>Gorgona, desecto vertentem lumina collo. <sup>439</sup>Tollite cuncta, inquit, coeptosque auferte labores, <sup>440</sup>Aetnaei Cyclopes, et huc advertite mentem: <sup>441</sup>arma acri facienda viro. Nunc viribus usus, <sup>442</sup>nunc manibus rapidis, omni nunc arte magistra. <sup>443</sup>Praecipitate moras. Nec plura effatus; at illi <sup>444</sup>ocius incubuere omnes pariterque laborem <sup>445</sup>sortiti. Fluit aes rivis aurique metallum, <sup>446</sup>volnificusque chalybs vasta fornace liquescit. <sup>447</sup>Ingentum clipeum informant, unum omnia contra <sup>448</sup>tela Latinorum, septenosque orbibus orbes <sup>449</sup>impediunt. Alii ventosis follibus auras <sup>450</sup>accipiunt redduntque, alii stridentia tingunt
translation.text: Sacred to Vulcan’s name, an isle there lay, Betwixt Sicilia’s coasts and Lipare, Rais’d high on smoking rocks; and, deep below, In hollow caves the fires of Aetna glow. The Cyclops here their heavy hammers deal; Loud strokes, and hissings of tormented steel, Are heard around; the boiling waters roar, And smoky flames thro’ fuming tunnels soar. Hither the Father of the Fire, by night, Thro’ the brown air precipitates his flight. On their eternal anvils here he found The brethren beating, and the blows go round. A load of pointless thunder now there lies Before their hands, to ripen for the skies: These darts, for angry Jove, they daily cast; Consum’d on mortals with prodigious waste. Three rays of writhen rain, of fire three more, Of winged southern winds and cloudy store As many parts, the dreadful mixture frame; And fears are added, and avenging flame. Inferior ministers, for Mars, repair His broken axletrees and blunted war, And send him forth again with furbish’d arms, To wake the lazy war with trumpets’ loud alarms. The rest refresh the scaly snakes that fold The shield of Pallas, and renew their gold. Full on the crest the Gorgon’s head they place, With eyes that roll in death, and with distorted face.

translation.original_text: <sup>451</sup>aera lacu. Gemit impositis incudibus antrum. <sup>452</sup>Illi inter sese multa vi bracchia tollunt <sup>453</sup>in numerum versantque tenaci forcipe massam. <sup>454</sup>Haec pater Aeoliis properat dum Lemnius oris, <sup>455</sup>Euandrum ex humili tecto lux suscitat alma <sup>456</sup>et matutini volucrum sub culmine cantus. <sup>457</sup>Consurgit senior tunicaque inducitur artus <sup>458</sup>et Tyrrhena pedum circumdat vincula plantis; <sup>459</sup>tum lateri atque umeris Tegeaeum subligat ensem, <sup>460</sup>demissa ab laeva pantherae terga retorquens. <sup>461</sup>Nec non et gemini custodes limine ab alto <sup>462</sup>praecedunt gressumque canes comitantur erilem. <sup>463</sup>Hospitis Aeneae sedem et secreta petebat <sup>464</sup>sermonum memor et promissi muneris heros. <sup>465</sup>Nec minus Aeneas se matutinus agebat. <sup>466</sup>Filius huic Pallas, illi comes ibat Achates. <sup>467</sup>Congressi iungunt dextras mediisque residunt <sup>468</sup>aedibus et licito tandem sermone fruuntur. <sup>469</sup>Rex prior haec: <sup>470</sup>Maxume Teucrorum ductor, quo sospite numquam <sup>471</sup>res equidem Troiae victas aut regna fatebor, <sup>472</sup>nobis ad belli auxilium pro nomine tanto <sup>473</sup>exiguae vires: hinc Tusco claudimur amni, <sup>474</sup>hinc Rutulus premit et murum circumsonat armis. <sup>475</sup>Sed tibi ego ingentis populos opulentaque regnis

<sup>476</sup>iungere castra paro, quam fors inopina salutem <sup>477</sup>ostentat: fatis huc te poscentibus adfers. <sup>478</sup>Haud procul hinc saxo incolitur fundata vetusto <sup>479</sup>urbis Agyllinae sedes, ubi Lydia quondam <sup>480</sup>gens, bello praeclara, iugis insedit Etruscis. <sup>481</sup>Hanc multos florentem annos rex deinde superbo <sup>482</sup>imperio et saevis tenuit Mezentius armis. <sup>483</sup>Quid memorem infandas caedes, quid facta tyranni <sup>484</sup>effera? Di capiti ipsius generique reservent! <sup>485</sup>Mortua quin etiam iungebat corpora vivis <sup>486</sup>componens manibusque manus atque oribus ora, <sup>487</sup>tormenti genus, et sanie taboque fluentis <sup>488</sup>complexu in misero longa sic morte necabat. <sup>489</sup>at fessi tandem cives infanda furentem <sup>490</sup>armati circumsistunt ipsumque domumque, <sup>491</sup>obtruncant socios, ignem ad fastigia iactant. <sup>492</sup>Ille inter caedem Rutulorum elapsus in agros <sup>493</sup>confugere et Turni defendier hospitis armis. <sup>494</sup>Ergo omnis furiis surrexit Etruria iustis: <sup>495</sup>regem ad supplicium praesenti Marte reposcunt. <sup>496</sup>his ego te, Aenea, ductorem milibus addam. <sup>497</sup>Toto namque fremunt condensae litore puppes <sup>498</sup>Signaque ferre iubent; retinet longaevus haruspex <sup>499</sup>fata canens, O Maeoniae delecta iuventus, <sup>500</sup>flos veterum virtusque virum, quos iustus in hostem
translation.text: “My sons,” said Vulcan, “set your tasks aside; Your strength and master-skill must now be tried. Arms for a hero forge; arms that require Your force, your speed, and all your forming fire.” He said. They set their former work aside, And their new toils with eager haste divide. A flood of molten silver, brass, and gold, And deadly steel, in the large furnace roll’d; Of this, their artful hands a shield prepare, Alone sufficient to sustain the war. Sev’n orbs within a spacious round they close: One stirs the fire, and one the bellows blows. The hissing steel is in the smithy drown’d; The grot with beaten anvils groans around. By turns their arms advance, in equal time; By turns their hands descend, and hammers chime. They turn the glowing mass with crooked tongs; The fiery work proceeds, with rustic songs.

translation.original_text: <sup>501</sup>fert dolor et merita accendit Mezentius ira, <sup>502</sup>nulli fas Italo tantam subiungere gentem: <sup>503</sup>externos optate duces; tum Etrusca resedit <sup>504</sup>hoc acies campo, monitis exterrita divom. <sup>505</sup>Ipse oratores ad me regnique coronam <sup>506</sup>cum sceptro misit mandatque insignia Tarchon, <sup>507</sup>succedam castris Tyrrhenaque regna capessam. <sup>508</sup>Sed mihi tarda gelu saeclisque effeta senectus <sup>509</sup>invidet imperium seraeque ad fortia vires. <sup>510</sup>natum exhortarer, ni mixtus matre Sabella <sup>511</sup>hinc partem patriae traheret. Tu, cuius et annis <sup>512</sup>et generi fatum indulgent, quem numina poscunt, <sup>513</sup>ingredere, o Teucrum atque Italum fortissime ductor. <sup>514</sup>hunc tibi praeterea, spes et solacia nostri, <sup>515</sup>Pallanta adiungam; sub te tolerare magistro <sup>516</sup>militiam et grave Martis opus, tua cernere facta <sup>517</sup>adsuescat primis et te miretur ab annis. <sup>518</sup>Arcadas huic equites bis centum, robora pubis <sup>519</sup>lecta dabo totidemque suo tibi nomine Pallas. <sup>520</sup>Vix ea fatus erat, defixique ora tenebant <sup>521</sup>Aeneas Anchisiades et fidus Achates <sup>522</sup>multaque dura suo tristi cum corde putabant, <sup>523</sup>ni signum caelo Cytherea dedisset aperto. <sup>524</sup>Namque inproviso vibratus ab aethere fulgor <sup>525</sup>cum sonitu venit, et ruere omnia visa repente
translation.text: While, at the Lemnian god’s command, they urge Their labours thus, and ply th’ Aeolian forge, The cheerful morn salutes Evander’s eyes, And songs of chirping birds invite to rise. He leaves his lowly bed: his buskins meet Above his ankles; sandals sheathe his feet: He sets his trusty sword upon his side, And o’er his shoulder throws a panther’s hide. Two menial dogs before their master press’d. Thus clad, and guarded thus, he seeks his kingly guest. Mindful of promis’d aid, he mends his pace, But meets Aeneas in the middle space. Young Pallas did his father’s steps attend, And true Achates waited on his friend. They join their hands; a secret seat they choose; Th’ Arcadian first their former talk renews: “Undaunted prince, I never can believe The Trojan empire lost, while you survive. Command th’ assistance of a faithful friend; But feeble are the succours I can send. Our narrow kingdom here the Tiber bounds; That other side the Latian state surrounds, Insults our walls, and wastes our fruitful grounds. But mighty nations I prepare, to join Their arms with yours, and aid your just design. You come, as by your better genius sent, And fortune seems to favour your intent. Not far from hence there stands a hilly town, Of ancient building, and of high renown, Torn from the Tuscans by the Lydian race, Who gave the name of Caere to the place, Once Agyllina call’d. It flourish’d long, In pride of wealth and warlike people strong, Till curs’d Mezentius, in a fatal hour, Assum’d the crown, with arbitrary pow’r. What words can paint those execrable times, The subjects’ suff’rings, and the tyrant’s crimes! That blood, those murders, O ye gods, replace On his own head, and on his impious race! The living and the dead at his command Were coupled, face to face, and hand to hand, Till, chok’d with stench, in loath’d embraces tied, The ling’ring wretches pin’d away and died. Thus plung’d in ills, and meditating more— The people’s patience, tir’d, no longer bore The raging monster; but with arms beset His house, and vengeance and destruction threat. They fire his palace: while the flame ascends, They force his guards, and execute his friends. He cleaves the crowd, and, favour’d by the night, To Turnus’ friendly court directs his flight. By just revenge the Tuscans set on fire, With arms, their king to punishment require: Their num’rous troops, now muster’d on the strand, My counsel shall submit to your command. Their navy swarms upon the coasts; they cry To hoist their anchors, but the gods deny. An ancient augur, skill’d in future fate, With these foreboding words restrains their hate: ‘Ye brave in arms, ye Lydian blood, the flow’r Of Tuscan youth, and choice of all their pow’r, Whom just revenge against Mezentius arms, To seek your tyrant’s death by lawful arms; Know this: no native of our land may lead This pow’rful people; seek a foreign head.’ Aw’d with these words, in camps they still abide, And wait with longing looks their promis’d guide. Tarchon, the Tuscan chief, to me has sent Their crown, and ev’ry regal ornament: The people join their own with his desire; And all my conduct, as their king, require. But the chill blood that creeps within my veins, And age, and listless limbs unfit for pains, And a soul conscious of its own decay, Have forc’d me to refuse imperial sway. My Pallas were more fit to mount the throne, And should, but he’s a Sabine mother’s son, And half a native; but, in you, combine A manly vigour, and a foreign line. Where Fate and smiling Fortune shew the way, Pursue the ready path to sov’reign sway. The staff of my declining days, my son, Shall make your good or ill success his own; In fighting fields from you shall learn to dare, And serve the hard apprenticeship of war; Your matchless courage and your conduct view, And early shall begin t’ admire and copy you. Besides, two hundred horse he shall command; Tho’ few, a warlike and well-chosen band. These in my name are listed; and my son As many more has added in his own.”

translation.original_text: <sup>526</sup>Tyrrhenusque tubae mugire per aethera clangor. <sup>527</sup>Suspiciunt, iterum atque iterum fragor increpat ingens: <sup>528</sup>arma inter nubem caeli regione serena <sup>529</sup>per sudum rutilare vident et pulsa tonare. <sup>530</sup>Obstipuere animis alii, sed Troius heros <sup>531</sup>agnovit sonitum et divae promissa parentis. <sup>532</sup>Tum memorat: Ne vero, hospes, ne quaere profecto, <sup>533</sup>quem casum portenta ferant: ego poscor Olympo. <sup>534</sup>Hoc signum cecinit missuram diva creatrix, <sup>535</sup>si bellum ingrueret, Volcaniaque arma per auras <sup>536</sup>laturam auxilio. <sup>537</sup>Heu quantae miseris caedes Laurentibus instant; <sup>538</sup>quas poenas mihi, Turne, dabis; quam multa sub undas <sup>539</sup>scuta virum galeasque et fortia corpora volves, <sup>540</sup>Thybri pater! Poscant acies et foedera rumpant. <sup>541</sup>Haec ubi dicta dedit, solio se tollit ab alto <sup>542</sup>et primum Herculeis sopitas ignibus aras <sup>543</sup>excitat hesternumque larem parvosque penatis <sup>544</sup>laetus adit: mactant lectas de more bidentis <sup>545</sup>Euandrus pariter, pariter Troiana iuventus. <sup>546</sup>Post hinc ad navis graditur sociosque revisit. <sup>547</sup>Quorum de numero qui sese in bella sequantur <sup>548</sup>praestantis virtute legit; pars cetera prona <sup>549</sup>fertur aqua segnisque secundo defluit amni, <sup>550</sup>nuntia ventura Ascanio rerumque patrisque.
translation.text: Scarce had he said; Achates and his guest, With downcast eyes, their silent grief express’d; Who, short of succours, and in deep despair, Shook at the dismal prospect of the war. But his bright mother, from a breaking cloud, To cheer her issue, thunder’d thrice aloud; Thrice forky lightning flash’d along the sky, And Tyrrhene trumpets thrice were heard on high. Then, gazing up, repeated peals they hear; And, in a heav’n serene, refulgent arms appear: Redd’ning the skies, and glitt’ring all around, The temper’d metals clash, and yield a silver sound. The rest stood trembling, struck with awe divine; Aeneas only, conscious to the sign, Presag’d th’ event, and joyful view’d, above, Th’ accomplish’d promise of the Queen of Love. Then, to th’ Arcadian king: “This prodigy (Dismiss your fear) belongs alone to me. Heav’n calls me to the war: th’ expected sign Is giv’n of promis’d aid, and arms divine. My goddess mother, whose indulgent care Foresaw the dangers of the growing war, This omen gave, when bright Vulcanian arms, Fated from force of steel by Stygian charms, Suspended, shone on high: she then foreshow’d Approaching fights, and fields to float in blood. Turnus shall dearly pay for faith forsworn; And corps, and swords, and shields, on Tiber borne, Shall choke his flood: now sound the loud alarms; And, Latian troops, prepare your perjur’d arms.”

translation.original_text: <sup>551</sup>Dantur equi Teucris Tyrrhena petentibus arva; <sup>552</sup>ducunt exsortem Aeneae, quem fulva leonis <sup>553</sup>pellis obit totum, praefulgens unguibus aureis. <sup>554</sup>Fama volat parvam subito volgata per urbem, <sup>555</sup>ocius ire equites Tyrrheni ad litora regis. <sup>556</sup>vota metu duplicant matres, propiusque periclo <sup>557</sup>it timor, et maior Martis iam apparet imago. <sup>558</sup>tum pater Evandrus dextram complexus euntis <sup>559</sup>haeret inexpletus lacrimans, ac talia fatur: <sup>560</sup>O mihi praeteritos referat si Iuppiter annos, <sup>561</sup>(qualis eram, cum primam aciem Praeneste sub ipsa <sup>562</sup>stravi scutorumque incendi victor acervos <sup>563</sup>et regem hac Erulum dextra sub Tartara misi, <sup>564</sup>nascenti cui tris animas Feronia mater <sup>565</sup>(horrendum dictu) dederat; terna arma movenda, <sup>566</sup>ter Leto sternendus erat; cui tunc tamen omnis <sup>567</sup>abstulit haec animas dextra et totidem exuit armis: <sup>568</sup>non ego nunc dulci amplexu divellerer usquam, <sup>569</sup>nate, tuo, neque finitimo Mezentius umquam <sup>570</sup>huic capiti insultans tot ferro saeva dedisset <sup>571</sup>funera, tam multis viduasset civibus urbem. <sup>572</sup>At vos, o superi, et divom tu maxume rector <sup>573</sup>Iuppiter, Arcadii, quaeso, miserescite regis <sup>574</sup>et patrias audite preces. Si numina vestra <sup>575</sup>incolumem Pallanta mihi, si fata reservant,
translation.text: He said, and, rising from his homely throne, The solemn rites of Hercules begun, And on his altars wak’d the sleeping fires; Then cheerful to his household gods retires; There offers chosen sheep. Th’ Arcadian king And Trojan youth the same oblations bring. Next, of his men and ships he makes review; Draws out the best and ablest of the crew. Down with the falling stream the refuse run, To raise with joyful news his drooping son. Steeds are prepar’d to mount the Trojan band, Who wait their leader to the Tyrrhene land. A sprightly courser, fairer than the rest, The king himself presents his royal guest: A lion’s hide his back and limbs infold, Precious with studded work, and paws of gold. Fame thro’ the little city spreads aloud Th’ intended march, amid the fearful crowd: The matrons beat their breasts, dissolve in tears, And double their devotion in their fears. The war at hand appears with more affright, And rises ev’ry moment to the sight.

translation.original_text: <sup>576</sup>si visurus eum vivo et venturus in unum: <sup>577</sup>vitam oro, patior quemvis durare laborem. <sup>578</sup>Sin aliquem infandum casum, Fortuna, minaris: <sup>579</sup>nunc O nunc liceat crudelem abrumpere vitam, <sup>580</sup>dum curae ambiguae, dum spes incerta futuri, <sup>581</sup>dum te, care puer, mea sera et sola voluptas, <sup>582</sup>complexu teneo, gravior neu nuntius auris <sup>583</sup>volneret. Haec genitor digressu dicta supremo <sup>584</sup>fundebat: famuli conlapsum in tecta ferebant. <sup>585</sup>Iamque adeo exierat portis equitatus apertis, <sup>586</sup>Aeneas inter primos et fidus Achates, <sup>587</sup>inde alii Troiae proceres, ipse agmine Pallas <sup>588</sup>in medio chlamyde et pictis conspectus in armis: <sup>589</sup>qualis ubi Oceani perfusus Lucifer unda, <sup>590</sup>quem Venus ante alios astrorum diligit ignis, <sup>591</sup>extulit os sacrum caelo tenebrasque resolvit. <sup>592</sup>Stant pavidae in muris matres oculisque sequuntur <sup>593</sup>pulveream nubem et fulgentis aere catervas. <sup>594</sup>Olli per dumos, qua proxuma meta viarum, <sup>595</sup>armati tendunt; it clamor, et agmine facto <sup>596</sup>quadrupedante putrem sonitu quatit ungula campum. <sup>597</sup>Est ingens gelidum lucus prope Caeritis amnem, <sup>598</sup>religione patrum late sacer; undique colles <sup>599</sup>inclusere cavi et nigra nemus abiete cingunt. <sup>600</sup>Silvano fama est veteres sacrasse Pelasgos,
translation.text: Then old Evander, with a close embrace, Strain’d his departing friend; and tears o’erflow his face. “Would Heav’n,” said he, “my strength and youth recall, Such as I was beneath Praeneste’s wall; Then when I made the foremost foes retire, And set whole heaps of conquer’d shields on fire; When Herilus in single fight I slew, Whom with three lives Feronia did endue; And thrice I sent him to the Stygian shore, Till the last ebbing soul return’d no more— Such if I stood renew’d, not these alarms, Nor death, should rend me from my Pallas’ arms; Nor proud Mezentius, thus unpunish’d, boast His rapes and murders on the Tuscan coast. Ye gods, and mighty Jove, in pity bring Relief, and hear a father and a king! If fate and you reserve these eyes, to see My son return with peace and victory; If the lov’d boy shall bless his father’s sight; If we shall meet again with more delight; Then draw my life in length; let me sustain, In hopes of his embrace, the worst of pain. But if your hard decrees—which, O! I dread— Have doom’d to death his undeserving head; This, O this very moment, let me die! While hopes and fears in equal balance lie; While, yet possess’d of all his youthful charms, I strain him close within these aged arms; Before that fatal news my soul shall wound!” He said, and, swooning, sunk upon the ground. His servants bore him off, and softly laid His languish’d limbs upon his homely bed.

translation.original_text: <sup>601</sup>arvorum pecorisque deo, lucumque diemque, <sup>602</sup>qui primi finis aliquando habuere Latinos: <sup>603</sup>haud procul hinc Tarcho et Tyrrheni tuta tenebant <sup>604</sup>castra locis, celsoque omnis de colle videri <sup>605</sup>iam poterat legio et latis tendebat in arvis. <sup>606</sup>Huc pater Aeneas et bello lecta iuventus <sup>607</sup>succedunt fessique et equos et corpora curant. <sup>608</sup>At Venus aetherios inter dea candida nimbos <sup>609</sup>dona ferens aderat; natumque in valle reducta <sup>610</sup>ut procul egelido secretum flumine vidit, <sup>611</sup>talibus adfata est dictis seque obtulit ultro: <sup>612</sup>En perfecta mei promissa coniugis arte <sup>613</sup>munera, ne mox aut Laurentis, nate, superbos <sup>614</sup>aut acrem dubites in proelia poscere Turnum. <sup>615</sup>Dixit et amplexus nati Cytherea petivit, <sup>616</sup>arma sub adversa posuit radiantia quercu. <sup>617</sup>Ille, deae donis et tanto laetus honore, <sup>618</sup>expleri nequit atque oculos per singula volvit <sup>619</sup>miraturque interque manus et bracchia versat <sup>620</sup>terribilem cristis galeam flammasque vomentem <sup>621</sup>fatiferumque ensem, loricam ex aere rigentem <sup>622</sup>sanguineam ingentem, qualis cum caerula nubes <sup>623</sup>solis inardescit radiis longeque refulget; <sup>624</sup>tum levis ocreas electro auroque recocto <sup>625</sup>hastamque et clipei non enarrabile textum.
translation.text: The horsemen march; the gates are open’d wide; Aeneas at their head, Achates by his side. Next these, the Trojan leaders rode along; Last follows in the rear th’ Arcadian throng. Young Pallas shone conspicuous o’er the rest; Gilded his arms, embroider’d was his vest. So, from the seas, exerts his radiant head The star by whom the lights of heav’n are led; Shakes from his rosy locks the pearly dews, Dispels the darkness, and the day renews. The trembling wives the walls and turrets crowd, And follow, with their eyes, the dusty cloud, Which winds disperse by fits, and shew from far The blaze of arms, and shields, and shining war. The troops, drawn up in beautiful array, O’er heathy plains pursue the ready way. Repeated peals of shouts are heard around; The neighing coursers answer to the sound, And shake with horny hoofs the solid ground.

translation.original_text: <sup>626</sup>Illic res Italas Romanorumque triumphos <sup>627</sup>haud vatum ignarus venturique inscius aevi <sup>628</sup>fecerat ignipotens, illic genus omne futurae <sup>629</sup>stirpis ab Ascanio. pugnataque in ordine bella. <sup>630</sup>Fecerat et viridi fetam Mavortis in antro <sup>631</sup>procubuisse lupam, geminos huic ubera circum <sup>632</sup>ludere pendentis pueros et lambere matrem <sup>633</sup>impavidos, illam tereti cervice reflexa <sup>634</sup>mulcere alternos et corpora fingere lingua. <sup>635</sup>Nec procul hinc Romam et raptas sine more Sabinas <sup>636</sup>consessu caveae magnis circensibus actis <sup>637</sup>addiderat subitoque novum consurgere bellum <sup>638</sup>Romulidis Tatioque seni Curibusque severis. <sup>639</sup>Post idem inter se posito certamine reges <sup>640</sup>armati Iovis ante aram paterasque tenentes <sup>641</sup>stabant et caesa iungebant foedera porca. <sup>642</sup>Haud procul inde citae Mettum in diversa quadrigae <sup>643</sup>distulerant, at tu dictis, Albane, maneres, <sup>644</sup>raptabatque viri mendacis viscera Tullus <sup>645</sup>per silvam, et sparsi rorabant sanguine vepres. <sup>646</sup>Nec non Tarquinium eiectum Porsenna iubebat <sup>647</sup>accipere ingentique urbem obsidione premebat: <sup>648</sup>Aeneadae in ferrum pro libertate ruebant. <sup>649</sup>Illum indignanti similem similemque minanti <sup>650</sup>aspiceres, pontem auderet quia vellere Cocles
translation.text: A greenwood shade, for long religion known, Stands by the streams that wash the Tuscan town, Incompass’d round with gloomy hills above, Which add a holy horror to the grove. The first inhabitants of Grecian blood, That sacred forest to Silvanus vow’d, The guardian of their flocks and fields; and pay Their due devotions on his annual day. Not far from hence, along the river’s side, In tents secure, the Tuscan troops abide, By Tarchon led. Now, from a rising ground, Aeneas cast his wond’ring eyes around, And all the Tyrrhene army had in sight, Stretch’d on the spacious plain from left to right. Thither his warlike train the Trojan led, Refresh’d his men, and wearied horses fed.

translation.original_text: <sup>651</sup>et fluvium vinclis innaret Cloelia ruptis. <sup>652</sup>In summo custos Tarpeiae Manlius arcis <sup>653</sup>stabat pro templo et Capitolia celsa tenebat, <sup>654</sup>Romuleoque recens horrebat regia culmo. <sup>655</sup>Atque hic auratis volitans argenteus anser <sup>656</sup>porticibus Gallos in limine adesse canebat. <sup>657</sup>Galli per dumos aderant arcemque tenebant, <sup>658</sup>defensi tenebris et dono noctis opacae: <sup>659</sup>aurea caesaries ollis atque aurea vestis, <sup>660</sup>virgatis lucent sagulis, tum lactea colla <sup>661</sup>auro innectuntur, duo quisque Alpina coruscant <sup>662</sup>gaesa manu, scutis protecti corpora longis. <sup>663</sup>Hic exsultantis Salios nudosque Lupercos <sup>664</sup>lanigerosque apices et lapsa ancilia caelo <sup>665</sup>extuderat, castae ducebant sacra per urbem <sup>666</sup>pilentis matres in mollibus. Hinc procul addit <sup>667</sup>Tartareas etiam sedes, alta ostia Ditis, <sup>668</sup>et scelerum poenas et te, Catilina, minaci <sup>669</sup>pendentem scopulo Furiarumque ora trementem, <sup>670</sup>secretosque pios, his dantem iura Catonem. <sup>671</sup>Haec inter tumidi late maris ibat imago <sup>672</sup>aurea, sed fluctu spumabant caerula cano; <sup>673</sup>et circum argento clari delphines in orbem <sup>674</sup>aequora verrebant caudis aestumque secabant. <sup>675</sup>In medio classis aeratas, Actia bella,
translation.text: Meantime the mother goddess, crown’d with charms, Breaks thro’ the clouds, and brings the fated arms. Within a winding vale she finds her son, On the cool river’s banks, retir’d alone. She shews her heav’nly form without disguise, And gives herself to his desiring eyes. “Behold,” she said, “perform’d in ev’ry part, My promise made, and Vulcan’s labour’d art. Now seek, secure, the Latian enemy, And haughty Turnus to the field defy.” She said; and, having first her son embrac’d, The radiant arms beneath an oak she plac’d, Proud of the gift, he roll’d his greedy sight Around the work, and gaz’d with vast delight. He lifts, he turns, he poises, and admires The crested helm, that vomits radiant fires: His hands the fatal sword and corslet hold, One keen with temper’d steel, one stiff with gold: Both ample, flaming both, and beamy bright; So shines a cloud, when edg’d with adverse light. He shakes the pointed spear, and longs to try The plated cuishes on his manly thigh; But most admires the shield’s mysterious mould, And Roman triumphs rising on the gold: For these, emboss’d, the heav’nly smith had wrought (Not in the rolls of future fate untaught) The wars in order, and the race divine Of warriors issuing from the Julian line. The cave of Mars was dress’d with mossy greens: There, by the wolf, were laid the martial twins. Intrepid on her swelling dugs they hung; The foster dam loll’d out her fawning tongue: They suck’d secure, while, bending back her head, She lick’d their tender limbs, and form’d them as they fed. Not far from thence new Rome appears, with games Projected for the rape of Sabine dames. The pit resounds with shrieks; a war succeeds, For breach of public faith, and unexampled deeds. Here for revenge the Sabine troops contend; The Romans there with arms the prey defend. Wearied with tedious war, at length they cease; And both the kings and kingdoms plight the peace. The friendly chiefs before Jove’s altar stand, Both arm’d, with each a charger in his hand: A fatted sow for sacrifice is led, With imprecations on the perjur’d head. Near this, the traitor Metius, stretch’d between Four fiery steeds, is dragg’d along the green, By Tullus’ doom: the brambles drink his blood, And his torn limbs are left the vulture’s food.
